did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) with transfer learning (DenseNet121 and Xception) and an attention mechanism (CBAM), to classify eight types of skin cancers using the ISIC dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel feature fusion model using DenseNet121 and Xception with a CBAM attention mechanism for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (transfer learning, data augmentation), experiments on the ISIC dataset, and reports new quantitative results (97.84% accuracy), rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Feature Fusion with Attention Mechanism for Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2023 6th International Conference on Electrical Information and Communication Technology (EICT)
brief_summary: This study presents a deep learning-based method for the automatic classification of eight types of skin cancers using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The proposed model employs a feature fusion approach that combines two pretrained Convolutional Neural Networks, DenseNet121 and Xception, with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) to prioritize critical features. Transfer learning was utilized to enhance performance while reducing computational requirements, training only 4 million parameters over 30 epochs. The model was evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Matthews correlation coefficient on a test set split at a 20% ratio. The proposed architecture achieved a state-of-the-art accuracy of 97.84%, demonstrating superior performance compared to existing methods. These results highlight the potential of this lightweight, attention-based fusion model for reliable computer-aided dermatology diagnostics.

all_task_types.target: Eight types of skin cancers (actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, pigmented benign keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of skin cancer types using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (8 classes) with an accuracy of 97.84%. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin cancer classification' and 'categorizing eight types of skin cancers'.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 (International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2019)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset consisting of eight types of skin cancer images. The image size in the dataset is 450x600 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the model with a 70% split ratio.
use.validation: Used for validation with a 10% split ratio.
use.testing: Used for testing with a 20% split ratio.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Matthews correlation coefficient, with comparisons made via ablation studies and comparison tables against existing studies without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019.notes: The proposed model achieved state-of-the-art performance compared to existing studies. The best hyperparameters were identified as learning rate 0.001, Adam optimizer, Pretrained training type, and 30 epochs.

Comparison_with_Existing_Studies.notes: The proposed model (97.84% accuracy on 8 classes) outperformed Gouda et al. and Nie et al., and was comparable to Rashid et al. (98.20% on 2 classes). Keerthana et al. used a different dataset (ISBI).

architecture_used.main_model: Feature fusion model combining DenseNet121 and Xception with Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from ImageNet (pretrained DenseNet121 and Xception)

architecture_details.fusion_method: Feature fusion of extracted features from DenseNet121 and Xception
architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) comprising channel attention and spatial attention modules
architecture_details.feature_reduction: Global Average Pooling (GAP) to convert 2D feature maps to 1D feature vectors
architecture_details.classifier_head: Multi-layer perceptron with three fully connected (dense) layers and a dropout layer
architecture_details.activations: ReLU for the first two dense layers, Softmax for the final classification layer

training_details.data_augmentation: Image resizing, rescaling (0 to 1), unsharp masking

preprocessing_techniques: Unsharp masking for image quality enhancement

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images rotated up to 40 degrees either clockwise or counterclockwise.
Applied with a 20% maximum horizontal and vertical shift allowed for each image.
Applied with a maximum shear angle of 0.2.
Images randomly zoomed in and out within a 20% range.
Randomly chosen images were horizontally flipped to create mirror images.

conclusions: The proposed feature fusion model combining DenseNet121 and Xception with a Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM) achieved a state-of-the-art accuracy of 97.84% in classifying eight types of skin cancers on the ISIC 2019 dataset.

The model demonstrated high precision (0.9698), recall (0.9769), F1-score (0.9732), and Matthews correlation coefficient (0.9739), indicating its ability to reduce false positive predictions and produce reliable classifications.
Transfer learning was effective in reducing computational requirements, allowing the model to train only 4 million parameters over 30 epochs while maintaining high performance.
The ablation study identified optimal hyperparameters as a learning rate of 0.001, the Adam optimizer, pretrained training type, and 30 epochs.

The proposed lightweight method outperformed existing studies such as Gouda et al. (85.70% accuracy) and Nie et al. (89.48% accuracy) on the ISIC dataset, and was comparable to Rashid et al. (98.20% accuracy on a binary task).
The study highlights the potential of deep learning-based skin cancer classification for early detection and the creation of effective computer-aided dermatology diagnostic systems.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single public dataset (ISIC 2019) with no external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

Comparisons with existing studies are methodologically flawed due to heterogeneity: the proposed model classifies 8 classes, whereas key comparators (Rashid et al., Gouda et al.) performed binary classification (2 classes), and Keerthana et al. used a different dataset (ISBI).
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed model over existing methods.
The paper does not report the total number of images or class-wise distribution in the ISIC 2019 dataset, preventing assessment of class imbalance issues.

Data leakage risk exists as the paper does not explicitly confirm that patient-level separation was maintained during the 70:10:20 split, which is critical for dermatological datasets where multiple images may come from the same patient.
The model was trained for only 30 epochs, which may be insufficient to fully converge or assess stability compared to studies using more extensive training regimes.
The claim of 'state-of-the-art' performance is overstated given the unfair comparison with binary classification tasks and the lack of rigorous statistical validation.
